# MACRO REPORT — 2026-04-30
**Delivered:** 12:05 UTC | **Generated by:** Claude claude-sonnet-4-6

---

## EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

The dominant macro narrative for April 30, 2026 is the intersection of a prolonged U.S.-Iran conflict disrupting global energy flows and a Federal Reserve locked in a holding pattern as inflation resurges. The Fed held rates at 3.50–3.75% at what is widely considered Jerome Powell's final meeting as Chair, with incoming Chair Kevin Warsh expected to take the helm by June [Source: https://www.federalreserve.gov/newsevents/pressreleases/monetary20260429a.htm]. Brent crude's extraordinary -13.4% single-session move highlights extreme volatility in oil markets as traders price diplomatic signals surrounding the Strait of Hormuz — the single most important variable for today's session. Gold surged to $4,643 as safe-haven demand persists despite near-term volatility, while the JPY has strengthened sharply (-1.9% in USD/JPY) on BoJ hawkish signals and safe-haven flows [Source: https://www.globalbankingandfinance.com/asian-stocks-meander-ahead-tech-earnings-boj-awaits/].

---

## 1. GLOBAL MACRO OVERVIEW

The global macro backdrop is dominated by stagflationary cross-currents. The U.S. Federal Reserve held its benchmark federal funds rate steady at 3.50–3.75% at the April 29 FOMC meeting — its third consecutive pause — as policymakers grappled with rising inflation driven by the Iran war's energy shock alongside a softening labor market [Source: https://www.federalreserve.gov/newsevents/pressreleases/monetary20260429a.htm]. The effective fed funds rate stands at 3.64% [Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/FEDFUNDS], with the 10-year Treasury yield at 4.36% and the 2-year at 3.84%, creating a positive 52bp term spread as markets price a "higher-for-longer" outcome [Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/DGS10].

Inflation dynamics remain acutely challenging. CPI YoY stands at 3.32% as of March [Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CPIAUCSL], with PPI MoM at 1.78% [Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/PPIACO], and the war's energy pass-through has yet to fully materialize in official data. U.S. gas prices have hit a national average of $4.23 per gallon, up from $2.98 before the Iran conflict began [Source: https://www.newsweek.com/us-gas-prices-hit-4-23-average-map-shows-prices-per-state-11892399]. Ray Dalio has flagged a "stagflationary period" for the U.S. economy, while crop prices have hit multi-year highs as the Strait of Hormuz closure disrupts fertilizer supply chains [Source: https://www.agriculture.com/partners-crop-price-index-hits-highest-since-november-2023-11961018].

Earnings season provided a partial counterweight to geopolitical anxiety. Microsoft, Alphabet, Meta, and Amazon all beat expectations, with Microsoft's AI business reporting a $37 billion annual revenue run rate, up 123% YoY [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/markets/live/earnings-live-updates-sofi-stock-tumbles-alphabet-stock-jumps-as-magnificent-7-results-roll-in-111414129]. Apple reports after today's close, representing the final major "Magnificent 7" print of the season. S&P 500 futures point to modest gains of ~0.43% heading into the session [Source: https://www.wsj.com/livecoverage/stock-market-today-dow-sp-500-nasdaq-04-30-2026].

Central banks globally are in a defensive crouch. The Bank of England held rates at 3.75% on April 30, warning that inflation is "likely to be higher later this year" as energy pass-through effects emerge, while noting limited ability to offset these pressures via monetary policy [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/30/bank-of-england-april-2026-interest-rate-decision-iran-war.html]. The ECB remains on hold at 2%, though analysts at Schroders are warning of potential hikes in June–July if second-round inflationary effects materialize [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/29/europes-central-banks-in-wait-and-see-mode-on-interest-rates.html]. The VIX sits at 17.96, down 4.52% on the day, suggesting markets are absorbing the macro anxiety with relative composure despite elevated geopolitical risk [Source: Yahoo Finance (^VIX)].

---

## 2. CONFLICT / GEOPOLITICAL STATUS

**U.S.-Iran War — Active Conflict, Strait of Hormuz Closure**

- The U.S.-Iran war entered its ninth-plus week with the Strait of Hormuz remaining effectively closed; Goldman Sachs estimates exports through the chokepoint have fallen to just 4% of normal levels. [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/30/oil-prices-today-brent-wti-us-iran-war-trump.html]
- President Trump instructed aides to prepare for an extended blockade of Iranian exports, according to the Wall Street Journal citing U.S. officials, removing near-term hopes of diplomatic resolution. [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/30/oil-prices-today-brent-wti-us-iran-war-trump.html]
- Iran proposed reopening the Strait of Hormuz conditional on Washington lifting its blockade and ending hostilities; the White House acknowledged receiving the proposal but Trump is being briefed on new military options. [Source: https://www.wsj.com/livecoverage/stock-market-today-dow-sp-500-nasdaq-04-30-2026]
- International LNG prices have surged as a result of the closure; TTF rose to $14.80/MMBtu for the week ending April 24, up 35% from pre-closure levels, while Asian JKM benchmark rose 51% to $16.02/MMBtu. [Source: https://cleantechnica.com/2026/04/29/international-lng-prices-rise-amid-strait-of-hormuz-closure/]
- Israel's economy continues to show resilience despite the broader conflict, with the IMF forecasting 4.4% growth in 2026, though labor shortages and consumer weakness are emerging headwinds. [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/30/israel-economy-stock-market-tel-aviv-trump-netanyahu-gdp-us-inflation.html]
- Eurozone inflation expectations jumped sharply in March, a concern for the ECB as the energy pass-through risks becoming entrenched. [Source: https://www.wsj.com/economy/eurozone-inflation-expectations-jumped-in-march-but-pay-growth-outlook-unchanged-8d189e6b]

## 8. WEEK AHEAD / SCENARIO ANALYSIS

| SCENARIO | PROBABILITY | TRIGGER | BRENT | S&P 500 | BTC |
|----------|-------------|---------|-------|---------|-----|
| Bull | 20% | Iran peace deal / Hormuz reopening announced; Apple earnings blow-out; BoJ holds dovish | $88 | 7,400 | $82,000 |
| Base | 55% | Status quo: Iran stalemate continues, Fed on hold, Apple meets estimates, BoJ holds | $100 | 7,150 | $76,000 |
| Bear | 25% | Trump orders new Iran military strikes; oil spikes above $115; Brent re-accelerates; PCE hot surprise triggers rate-hike fears | $118 | 6,800 | $68,000 |

### Key Upcoming Catalysts

1. **April 30 (Today, Post-Close)** — Apple Q2 2026 Earnings: Final "Magnificent 7" report of the season; consensus expects $8.15 EPS on $55.5B revenue; miss risk could weigh on NDX and broader tech sentiment. [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/markets/live/earnings-live-updates-sofi-stock-tumbles-alphabet-stock-jumps-as-magnificent-7-results-roll-in-111414129]

2. **April 30 (Today, Ongoing)** — BoJ Governor Ueda Press Conference: Following a hawkish split vote that triggered a -1.9% USD/JPY move, Ueda's language on inflation and future rate hike timing is critical for JPY crosses and Nikkei direction. [Source: https://www.globalbankingandfinance.com/asian-stocks-meander-ahead-tech-earnings-boj-awaits/]

3. **May 1 (Friday)** — U.S. Core PCE Inflation (March): The Fed's preferred inflation gauge; any surprise above consensus given energy pass-through risks could push out rate-cut expectations further and pressure equities/gold. [Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CPIAUCSL]

4. **Week of May 4** — Kevin Warsh Senate Confirmation Hearings (Expected): Markets will scrutinize Warsh's testimony for signals on Fed independence, rate trajectory, and response to the Iran war's inflationary impact. [Source: https://www.cbsnews.com/news/fed-rate-decision-april-2026-powell-final-meeting/]

5. **Ongoing** — Iran-U.S. Diplomatic Developments / Hormuz Status: The most binary market risk; any credible de-escalation signal could trigger an immediate $10–$15/bbl Brent decline and equity rally, while escalation (new military action) would spike oil above $115 and trigger broad risk-off. [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/28/oil-prices-us-iran-hormuz-negotiations-wti-brent-crude.html]

---

### Disputed

- [Brent crude -13.41% single-session drop "from approximately $118 area"]: The data bundle shows Brent at $102.20 with a -13.41% change, implying a prior level of approximately $118. However, news sources from April 28 reference Brent at $108.23 (CNBC Daily Open) and "topping $111" (OilPrice.com April 28), with a separate CNBC article referencing "Brent at $120." The implied prior level of ~$118 is plausible but inconsistent across sources; the $120 CNBC headline appears to reference an intraday/futures spike, not a prior close. The exact prior close from which the -13.41% is calculated cannot be precisely confirmed from the bundle.

- [WTI/Brent relationship "has inverted temporarily"]: The data shows WTI ($104.35) trading above Brent ($102.20), which is indeed an inversion. However, the Axios/Mining.com data show Brent Crude at ~$104.4 and Crude Oil (WTI) at ~$101.85 in the mining.com price table, suggesting the spread direction may be data-dependent or reflect different contract maturities. The inversion claim is partially supported but not definitively confirmed.

- [BoJ "hawkish split vote" as the cause of USD/JPY -1.9% move]: The Global Banking & Finance source confirms the BoJ was "expected to hold rates steady" and references "a hawkish split at the Bank of Japan." However, the source does not confirm an actual split vote occurred—it describes expectations and a "hawkish split" that "underlined growing fears." The report presents this as confirmed fact when the source language is forward-looking/ambiguous.

- [Henry Hub "down about 9% from pre-conflict levels" as cited for the Neutral-Bear thesis]: The Axios source states Henry Hub is "down about 20% from last year," not 9% from pre-conflict. The 9% figure comes from the CleanTechnica source specifically referencing the period since February 28 (Hormuz closure). The report conflates these two different reference periods without clarification, creating a potential misreading of the bearish magnitude.

- [Andy Lipow estimate: "absent new negotiations, WTI will drift back to $100"]: The CNBC source from April 28 states Lipow said WTI "will drift back up to $100," implying upward drift back to $100, not downward to $100. Given WTI is currently at $104.35, this phrasing in the original source suggests Lipow was speaking when WTI was below $100, which contradicts the current price. The report uses this quote to suggest $100 is a floor, but the directional context from the source is ambiguous relative to current levels.

---

### Additional Insights

1. **Brent/WTI Spread Inversion Is a Tradeable Signal**: WTI ($104.35) is trading above Brent ($102.20), an unusual inversion. This spread dislocation—likely reflecting differential contract dynamics and U.S. landlocked supply cushion—historically mean-reverts. Traders can monitor for Brent re-convergence above WTI as Hormuz supply signals evolve; a re-widening of the normal Brent premium would signal market normalization and may precede broader oil price recovery or stabilization.

2. **U.S. Natural Gas Is a Structural Long-Short Divergence Play**: Henry Hub at $2.608 is down while TTF is at $14.80/MMBtu—a ~5.7x differential. The CleanTechnica source confirms the DOE approved two LNG export authorization increases (Plaquemines +0.5 Bcf/d, Elba Island +0.1 Bcf/d). Each additional export authorization approval is a direct positive catalyst for Henry Hub and a marginal negative for TTF (as more U.S. supply reaches Europe). Traders should monitor DOE export authorization news as a specific, actionable catalyst for this spread compression.

3. **FOMC Dissent Structure Signals Internal Fed Tension
